events.text: The patient is a 62-year-old female named Margaret Yuen. She is of East Asian (Chinese-American) descent, born in San Francisco, California. She is a former smoker with a 15 pack-year smoking history, having quit 8 years prior to diagnosis. She works as a retired schoolteacher. She has a family history of lung cancer (mother, deceased at age 72). Her performance status at initial presentation is ECOG 1.

At age 62 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0, AJCC 8th edition) NON-SMALL CELL LUNG CANCER, ADENOCARCINOMA (ICD-O-3 histology code 8140/3; primary site: upper lobe of left lung, C34.12). Relevant biomarkers included EGFR L858R point mutation (exon 21) confirmed positive by liquid biopsy and tissue NGS; PD-L1 TPS 15%; ALK and ROS1 negative by FISH; KRAS wild-type. Summary stage: Regional (N3 contralateral mediastinal and supraclavicular nodal involvement). Tumor size: 3.8 cm primary mass in left upper lobe with satellite nodule in the same lobe. Additional findings: contralateral right paratracheal (4R) and subcarinal (7) lymphadenopathy. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via CT-guided biopsy of the primary mass.

At age 62 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from CT-guided core needle biopsy of left upper lobe mass. Histology: Invasive adenocarcinoma, acinar and lepidic pattern predominant (60% acinar, 30% lepidic, 10% solid). Tumor cells show moderate nuclear pleomorphism. No lymphovascular invasion identified in biopsy specimen. Immunohistochemistry: TTF-1 positive (3+), Napsin A positive (2+), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, p40 negative, confirming pulmonary adenocarcinoma of primary lung origin. PD-L1 (22C3 PharmDx): TPS 15%, CPS 22. EGFR mutation testing (PCR-based): L858R mutation detected in exon 21. ALK (D5F3 IHC): Negative. ROS1 (IHC): Negative.

At age 62 years, the patient had a PET-CT (whole body FDG-PET with diagnostic CT) performed for initial staging. The study showed a hypermetabolic left upper lobe mass measuring 3.8 x 2.9 cm (SUVmax 12.4) with a 1.2 cm satellite nodule in the left upper lobe (SUVmax 6.8). Hypermetabolic contralateral right paratracheal lymph nodes (4R, largest 1.9 cm, SUVmax 9.1) and subcarinal lymph node (station 7, 1.4 cm, SUVmax 7.3). Left-sided hilar adenopathy (SUVmax 5.2). No distant metastatic disease. No evidence of brain involvement on limited PET-CT head views. The findings are consistent with Stage IIIB NSCLC.

At age 62 years, the patient had an MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST performed for CNS staging prior to treatment initiation. The study showed no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No mass effect or midline shift. Incidental finding of a small 4 mm meningioma-appearing extra-axial lesion at the right parietal convexity, without significant mass effect, to be followed. No acute intracranial abnormality otherwise.

At age 62 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, based on a tissue specimen obtained from a left upper lobe core needle biopsy. The panel (comprehensive solid tumor NGS, 500+ gene panel) showed: EGFR p.L858R mutation (exon 21, VAF 38%), STK11 p.Q37* nonsense mutation (VAF 22%), KEAP1 p.G480V missense mutation (VAF 19%), TP53 p.R248W missense mutation (VAF 31%), and CDKN2A homozygous deletion. Copy number alterations: MET amplification (low-level, copy number ratio 2.4), EGFR amplification (copy number ratio 4.1). No KRAS, BRAF, ALK, ROS1, MET exon 14 skipping, ERBB2, or RET alterations detected. TMB: 6 mutations/Mb (low). MSI: stable. No NTRK fusions detected. Germline pathogenic variants: none identified on somatic/germline comparison. The STK11 and KEAP1 co-mutations are noted as potentially associated with reduced immunotherapy efficacy.

At age 62 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board assessment. The case was reviewed by thoracic oncology, radiation oncology, and thoracic surgery. Given Stage IIIB disease with N3 (contralateral mediastinal) involvement, the patient is not a surgical candidate. The presence of EGFR L858R mutation was discussed at length. After review of LAURA trial data and consideration of concurrent chemoradiation followed by osimertinib consolidation, the team elected to treat with definitive chemoradiation followed by osimertinib maintenance given the strong EGFR driver. The STK11/KEAP1 co-mutations were noted as unfavorable for immunotherapy. ECOG PS 1. Cancer present. Plan: concurrent carboplatin/paclitaxel with thoracic radiation, followed by osimertinib consolidation/maintenance.

At age 62 years, the patient received definitive concurrent thoracic radiation therapy, 60 Gy in 30 fractions (2.0 Gy/fraction) to the left upper lobe primary tumor and involved mediastinal/hilar lymph nodes (including bilateral mediastinal stations 4R, 4L, 7, and left hilar). Technique: IMRT with daily IGRT (CBCT). Treatment delivered over 6 weeks concurrently with carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy. Gross tumor volume (GTV) to clinical target volume (CTV) expansion of 8 mm; PTV expansion 5 mm. Maximum spinal cord dose: 44 Gy. Mean lung dose: 18 Gy. V20: 28%. Treatment completed without unplanned interruptions.

At age 62 years, the patient received concurrent platinum-based chemotherapy with carboplatin AUC 2 and paclitaxel 45 mg/m2 weekly for 6 weeks, administered concurrently with thoracic radiation therapy. This was given as radiosensitizing chemotherapy for Stage IIIB NSCLC. No dose reductions were required. The patient tolerated treatment with manageable toxicities.

At age 62 years, during concurrent chemoradiation, the patient experienced Grade 2 radiation esophagitis (CTCAE v5.0) manifesting as odynophagia and dysphagia to solids, requiring dietary modification to liquid/soft diet and proton pump inhibitor therapy. She also experienced Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis symptoms (mild dry cough) and Grade 1 fatigue. Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (mild fingertip tingling) attributed to weekly paclitaxel. All toxicities resolved within 6 weeks of completing concurrent treatment.

At age 62 years (approximately 8 weeks post-completion of concurrent chemoradiation), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST as a post-treatment restaging study. The study showed significant interval decrease in the left upper lobe mass, now measuring 1.9 x 1.4 cm (from 3.8 x 2.9 cm, approximately 50% reduction). The left upper lobe satellite nodule was no longer visible. Right paratracheal (4R) lymph nodes decreased to 0.9 cm (from 1.9 cm). Subcarinal lymph node decreased to 0.7 cm (from 1.4 cm). Moderate post-radiation fibrotic changes in the left upper lobe parenchyma. No new thoracic or abdominal lesions. Post-treatment response consistent with partial response by RECIST 1.1. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 62 years (approximately 10 weeks after completing concurrent chemoradiation, following post-treatment restaging confirmation of response), the patient initiated osimertinib 80 mg orally once daily as consolidation/maintenance therapy for Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant NSCLC. This was prescribed based on LAURA trial data supporting osimertinib consolidation following definitive chemoradiation in Stage III unresectable EGFR-mutant NSCLC.

At age 63 years (approximately 6 months after starting osimertinib maintenance), the patient had a routine oncology follow-up assessment. The patient reports mild fatigue (Grade 1) and occasional diarrhea (Grade 1, 2-3 loose stools per day, self-managed with loperamide as needed). She denies dyspnea, hemoptysis, chest pain, headache, or neurological symptoms. ECOG PS 1. Physical exam: mild acneiform rash on face and chest (Grade 1). Prior imaging at 3-month mark showed continued partial response. Cancer present, responding to therapy. Plan: continue osimertinib 80 mg daily, repeat CT in 3 months, annual brain MRI.

At age 63 years (approximately 9 months after starting osimertinib maintenance), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST. The study showed further interval decrease in the residual left upper lobe opacity, now measuring 1.3 x 1.0 cm with surrounding post-radiation fibrotic changes. Right paratracheal lymph nodes stable at 0.7 cm (likely post-treatment/fibrotic). No new intrathoracic lesions. No abdominal or pelvic metastatic disease. Findings consistent with ongoing partial response to osimertinib. No evidence of progression.

At age 63 years (approximately 14 months after starting osimertinib maintenance), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST showing new concerning findings. The residual left upper lobe lesion has increased in size, now measuring 2.1 x 1.7 cm (from 1.3 x 1.0 cm). A new right lower lobe nodule measures 0.9 cm (new from prior). New subcentimeter right adrenal nodule (0.7 cm) is of indeterminate but suspicious nature. The findings are concerning for progression of disease after prior partial response. Cancer present, progressive disease by RECIST 1.1 criteria (>20% increase in index lesion, new lesions).

At age 63 years (same timeframe, approximately 14 months after osimertinib initiation), the patient had an MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST (gadolinium) urgently obtained given new systemic progression. The study showed two new intracranial enhancing lesions: a 1.4 cm right frontal lobe metastasis with surrounding vasogenic edema (approximately 2.8 cm of edema), and a 0.7 cm left cerebellar hemisphere metastasis. The right parietal extra-axial lesion noted previously remains stable and is not consistent with metastatic involvement. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No hydrocephalus. The findings confirm new brain metastases coinciding with systemic progression of EGFR-mutant NSCLC.

At age 63 years, the patient had a pathology result from CT-guided core needle biopsy of the new right lower lobe nodule performed to confirm progression and assess for resistance mechanisms. Histology: Metastatic adenocarcinoma, morphology consistent with known primary lung adenocarcinoma. Maintained TTF-1 positivity (3+). No histologic transformation to small cell carcinoma. PD-L1 TPS: 5% (decreased from 15% at initial diagnosis). EGFR mutation testing on this specimen confirmed L858R mutation retained, with additional T790M resistance mutation identified. This confirms acquired T790M-mediated resistance to osimertinib (noting that T790M as a resistance mechanism to osimertinib is less common than to 1st/2nd generation EGFR TKIs, but does occur in approximately 10-15% of osimertinib-resistant cases).

At age 63 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, at time of progression, based on a tissue specimen obtained from a right lower lobe core needle biopsy. The panel showed: EGFR p.L858R mutation (exon 21, VAF 41%), EGFR p.T790M mutation (exon 20, VAF 28%, newly acquired resistance mutation), STK11 p.Q37* (VAF 25%, retained from prior), KEAP1 p.G480V (VAF 22%, retained), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 35%, retained), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (retained). New alterations identified at progression: PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (VAF 12%, new, potential co-resistance mechanism), MET amplification increased (copy number ratio 5.8, increased from 2.4 at diagnosis, high-level amplification now present as a potential bypass resistance pathway). No EGFR C797S mutation detected. No ALK, ROS1, or RET rearrangements. No BRAF V600E. TMB: 8 mutations/Mb (low). Liquid biopsy (ctDNA) concordant findings with EGFR L858R, T790M, and MET amplification confirmed.

At age 63 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board and oncology assessment at the time of progression. Discussion points: (1) Brain metastases -- two lesions amenable to stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS); right frontal 1.4 cm and left cerebellar 0.7 cm; neurosurgery and radiation oncology consulted and agree SRS is appropriate over whole brain radiation given limited number of lesions and desire to preserve neurocognitive function. (2) Systemic progression with T790M and MET amplification as co-resistance mechanisms; no approved targeted therapy for this combination. (3) Plan: SRS to brain metastases first, then transition to platinum-based doublet chemotherapy. Consider clinical trial enrollment for MET amplification-driven resistance post-EGFR TKI. Osimertinib to be continued briefly until SRS completed, then discontinued. ECOG PS 1. Cancer present, progressive disease.

At age 63 years, the patient received stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to two brain metastases using a linear accelerator-based system (frameless, image-guided). Right frontal lobe metastasis (1.4 cm): 20 Gy in a single fraction. Left cerebellar hemisphere metastasis (0.7 cm): 22 Gy in a single fraction. Treatment was delivered in a single session with high-resolution MRI-based planning. Concurrent osimertinib was held 48 hours before and 72 hours after SRS per institutional protocol to minimize radiosensitization risk. The patient tolerated SRS well with no acute toxicity. Post-SRS dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily for 5 days prescribed for prophylactic management of cerebral edema.

At age 63 years, following SRS, the patient experienced transient Grade 1 headache and mild nausea lasting 3 days, well-controlled with dexamethasone taper and antiemetics. No seizures. No new neurological deficits. She was monitored for radiation necrosis at the SRS sites. Dexamethasone tapered over 2 weeks without complication.

At age 63 years (approximately 3 weeks after completing SRS to brain metastases), the patient initiated second-line systemic chemotherapy with carboplatin AUC 5 on day 1 and pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 on day 1, every 21 days, for progression of EGFR-mutant NSCLC after osimertinib. Pemetrexed supplementation with folic acid 400 mcg daily and vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks was initiated per standard protocol. No bevacizumab added given prior thoracic radiation and concern for pulmonary hemorrhage risk. ECOG PS 1. Osimertinib was discontinued at this time.

At age 63-64 years, during platinum/pemetrexed chemotherapy, the patient experienced Grade 2 nausea (requiring scheduled ondansetron and prochlorperazine), Grade 2 fatigue limiting some activities of daily living, Grade 1 alopecia (diffuse thinning), and Grade 1 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 98 x10^9/L, no dose reduction required). She also developed Grade 2 anemia (hemoglobin nadir 9.1 g/dL) requiring erythropoiesis-stimulating agent support and one unit pRBC transfusion after cycle 3. No febrile neutropenia. ECOG PS worsened transiently to 2 during cycles 2-3, recovering to PS 1 by cycle 4.

At age 64 years (after 4 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST for restaging. The left upper lobe lesion is now 1.8 x 1.4 cm (stable/minor decrease from 2.1 x 1.7 cm). Right lower lobe nodule decreased to 0.5 cm (from 0.9 cm). Right adrenal nodule now 0.6 cm (stable/slightly decreased). No new thoracic lesions. No new abdominal or pelvic metastatic sites. Findings consistent with stable disease/minor partial response to carboplatin/pemetrexed by RECIST 1.1 criteria. Cancer present, with partial response/stable disease to second-line chemotherapy.

At age 64 years (approximately 3 months after SRS, 3-month follow-up brain MRI), the patient had an MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST. The right frontal SRS-treated metastasis shows central necrosis and peripheral rim enhancement, consistent with post-treatment effect/radiation necrosis, now measuring 0.9 cm (decreased from 1.4 cm). The left cerebellar SRS-treated metastasis shows complete radiographic response (no longer visible). No new intracranial metastatic lesions. No leptomeningeal enhancement. The right parietal extra-axial meningioma-appearing lesion remains stable at 4 mm. Impression: Excellent intracranial response to SRS; post-treatment changes at right frontal site without evidence of active metastatic disease.

At age 64 years, following 6 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed (completing the doublet phase), the patient had an oncology assessment for transition to maintenance therapy. The patient tolerated treatment with the adverse events previously noted. Restaging shows stable disease/minor response systemically and excellent intracranial control post-SRS. However, given the presence of MET amplification as a co-resistance mechanism and progressive disease biology, the oncologist discussed clinical trial options. The patient was referred to a Phase I/II trial investigating a MET inhibitor (tepotinib) in combination with a 3rd-generation EGFR TKI in patients with EGFR-mutant NSCLC with MET amplification post-EGFR TKI resistance. Patient expressed strong interest in trial participation. ECOG PS 1. Cancer present, stable disease on current therapy.

At age 64 years (following 6 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed), the patient transitioned to pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 IV every 21 days as single-agent maintenance therapy, pending final eligibility determination for clinical trial enrollment. Continuation of folic acid and vitamin B12 supplementation. This transition was planned as a bridge to clinical trial enrollment while maintaining disease control.

At age 64 years, the patient was formally enrolled in a Phase I/II open-label clinical trial investigating tepotinib (MET inhibitor, 500 mg orally daily) in combination with osimertinib (80 mg orally daily) in patients with EGFR-mutant NSCLC harboring MET amplification as an acquired resistance mechanism to prior EGFR TKI therapy. Eligibility confirmed: EGFR L858R/T790M mutation, MET amplification (copy number ratio 5.8), ECOG PS 1, adequate organ function (creatinine clearance 78 mL/min, ALT/AST within normal limits, adequate bone marrow reserve). Informed consent obtained. Pemetrexed maintenance discontinued upon trial enrollment. Trial ID: NCT[REDACTED]. This represents enrollment onto a combination targeted therapy strategy addressing both the T790M resistance mechanism (osimertinib) and the MET amplification bypass pathway (tepotinib).

At age 64 years, during the first 8 weeks of tepotinib plus osimertinib clinical trial therapy, the patient experienced Grade 1 peripheral edema (bilateral lower extremity pitting edema, ankles) attributed to tepotinib (a known class effect of MET inhibitors), managed with compression stockings and sodium restriction. Grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools/day) attributed to osimertinib, managed with dietary modification. Grade 1 acneiform rash on face, chest, and upper back. No Grade 3 or higher toxicities. No QTc prolongation on ECG monitoring. ECOG PS 1 maintained.

At age 64 years (approximately 8 weeks after initiating clinical trial with tepotinib plus osimertinib), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST as the first on-trial restaging. The left upper lobe lesion has decreased to 1.2 x 0.9 cm (from 1.8 x 1.4 cm, a 35% reduction). Right lower lobe nodule no longer measurable (resolved or below threshold). Right adrenal nodule resolved. No new lesions identified in the thorax, abdomen, or pelvis. Findings are consistent with partial response by RECIST 1.1 criteria (>30% reduction in sum of target lesion diameters). Cancer present, responding to clinical trial therapy.

At age 64 years (6-month follow-up brain MRI on clinical trial), the patient had an MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST. The right frontal SRS site shows progressive reduction in size with the rim enhancement now minimal, measuring 0.5 cm, consistent with continued post-treatment evolution/radiation necrosis. No viable metastatic disease. No new intracranial metastatic lesions identified. No leptomeningeal disease. Right parietal extra-axial lesion remains stable. Impression: Sustained intracranial control with no evidence of new brain metastatic disease at 6 months post-SRS. No new CNS involvement on tepotinib/osimertinib combination.

At age 64 years (ongoing, approximately 4 months into clinical trial enrollment), the patient had a protocol-specified oncology assessment. She continues on tepotinib 500 mg daily and osimertinib 80 mg daily. She reports stable Grade 1 peripheral edema and occasional Grade 1 diarrhea, both manageable. She notes improved energy levels and has resumed light activities including walking and gardening. Repeat imaging shows confirmed partial response. CEA tumor marker has decreased from a peak of 42 ng/mL at time of progression to 8 ng/mL currently (normal <3.4 ng/mL, but trending toward normal). ECOG PS 1. She remains on clinical trial with continued disease control. Cancer present, responding to clinical trial combination targeted therapy. Next on-trial imaging and assessment scheduled in 6 weeks.

documents.text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
POST-TREATMENT RESTAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margaret Yuen, 62-year-old female with Stage IIIB left upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma (EGFR L858R-mutant). Study obtained approximately 8 weeks following completion of definitive concurrent chemoradiation (carboplatin/paclitaxel weekly x6 weeks with 60 Gy in 30 fractions IMRT to the left upper lobe primary and involved mediastinal/hilar nodal stations). Presented for post-treatment response assessment prior to initiation of consolidation therapy.

TECHNIQUE:
Multi-detector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed with intravenous contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL, injected at 3.5 mL/sec). Chest acquired in the portal venous phase (70 seconds); abdomen and pelvis acquired in the portal venous phase. 1.25 mm axial reconstructions reviewed in standard lung, mediastinal, and bone windows. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats generated and reviewed. No prior allergic reaction to contrast agents on file. No oral contrast administered.

Comparison: PET-CT performed at initial staging.

---

FINDINGS:

LUNGS AND AIRWAYS:
The left upper lobe mass has undergone substantial interval reduction in size and metabolic activity compared with the prior staging PET-CT. The mass now appears as an ill-defined area of consolidation and ground-glass opacity measuring approximately 1.9 x 1.4 cm in its greatest axial dimensions, compared to the original 3.8 x 2.9 cm hypermetabolic mass on staging PET-CT. The lesion retains some heterogeneous soft tissue density centrally but is now surrounded by adjacent streaky linear opacities and reticular changes that are in keeping with evolving post-radiation fibrotic remodeling of the left upper lobe parenchyma. The previously described 1.2 cm satellite nodule within the left upper lobe is no longer identified and is presumed resolved.

The left upper lobe shows moderate volume loss with irregular pleural tethering and linear bands of parenchymal scarring in the expected radiation field, consistent with post-treatment radiation-related changes. There is mild ipsilateral mediastinal shift and left major fissure displacement attributable to this fibrotic process.

The remaining left lung parenchyma shows no focal consolidation, nodularity, or infiltrate beyond the post-radiation field. No left-sided pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

The right lung is clear. No right-sided pleural effusion or nodularity. No endobronchial lesion. Trachea and central airways patent and midline.

MEDIASTINUM AND HILA:
The right paratracheal (station 4R) lymph node has markedly decreased in size and now measures approximately 0.9 cm in short axis (previously 1.9 cm at staging). The subcarinal (station 7) lymph node has similarly decreased and now measures approximately 0.7 cm (previously 1.4 cm). These nodes appear less bulky and conspicuous than on the prior study. There is some surrounding soft tissue stranding in the right paratracheal and subcarinal regions which likely reflects post-treatment inflammatory and fibrotic change rather than residual active nodal disease, though residual microscopic disease cannot be excluded.

Left hilar soft tissue fullness has decreased compared to the prior staging PET-CT; no discrete left hilar mass is now identifiable. No new mediastinal or hilar adenopathy. No pericardial effusion. Heart size normal.

THORACIC AORTA AND VESSELS:
No aortic aneurysm or dissection. Pulmonary arteries unremarkable. No evidence of pulmonary embolism on this portal venous phase acquisition.

CHEST WALL AND PLEURA:
No chest wall mass or rib lesion. No significant pleural thickening. Bilateral breasts without suspicious lesions.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: No focal hepatic lesion. Normal contour and parenchymal attenuation. Portal and hepatic veins patent.
Gallbladder and biliary system: Normal. No biliary ductal dilatation.
Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation.
Pancreas: Normal appearance. No ductal dilatation or peripancreatic fat stranding.
Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits. No adrenal nodule or mass identified.
Kidneys: No renal mass or hydronephrosis. No nephrolithiasis. Normal cortical enhancement bilaterally.
Bowel: No small or large bowel obstruction or wall thickening. No free intraperitoneal fluid or air.
Retroperitoneum and mesentery: No pathologic lymphadenopathy in the retroperitoneum, para-aortic, or mesenteric chains.

PELVIS:
Uterus and ovaries not visualized (patient is post-menopausal; age-appropriate). No pelvic mass or free fluid. No pathologic pelvic lymphadenopathy. Urinary bladder not fully distended; no gross wall thickening.

BONE AND SOFT TISSUES:
Visualized osseous structures without lytic or sclerotic lesion suspicious for metastatic involvement. No pathologic fracture. No worrisome soft tissue mass.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Marked interval reduction in size of the left upper lobe primary mass, now measuring approximately 1.9 x 1.4 cm, representing a substantial decrease from the 3.8 x 2.9 cm mass documented at initial staging. This is accompanied by surrounding post-radiation parenchymal fibrosis and volume loss in the left upper lobe, consistent with the known radiation field.

2. Complete resolution of the previously identified left upper lobe satellite nodule.

3. Considerable decrease in the right paratracheal (4R) and subcarinal (station 7) nodal disease, with the 4R node now approximately 0.9 cm and subcarinal node approximately 0.7 cm -- both markedly smaller than at initial staging. Surrounding soft tissue stranding likely reflects post-treatment fibroinflammatory change.

4. Resolution of previously noted left hilar adenopathy.

5. No new intrathoracic, abdominal, or pelvic lesions to suggest interval development of distant metastatic disease.

6. No adrenal, hepatic, osseous, or lymph node abnormality suspicious for hematogenous spread.

Overall, the imaging findings demonstrate a robust treatment response in the chest following concurrent chemoradiation, with significant reduction in the primary tumor burden and nodal disease. Post-radiation parenchymal changes are expected and prominent in the left upper lobe. No evidence of new distant spread.

Reviewing Radiologist: [SIGNATURE BLOCK REDACTED]
Attending Radiologist, Thoracic Imaging Section

THORACIC ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP / MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD NOTE

ATTENDING: [Attending Thoracic Oncologist]
CC: Dr. [Radiation Oncology], Dr. [Neurosurgery] -- copy for tumor board documentation

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Ms. Margaret Yuen is a 63-year-old Chinese-American female with Stage IIIB NSCLC adenocarcinoma (EGFR L858R exon 21) presenting today for urgent multidisciplinary review following imaging-confirmed systemic and intracranial disease progression on osi. This visit incorporates findings from recent cross-sectional and brain imaging as well as rebiopsy results obtained from the new right lower lobe nodule.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Yuen is well known to our practice. She was originally diagnosed with left upper lobe NSCLC adenocarcinoma (T3N3M0, Stage IIIB) at age 62. Initial biomarker profile was notable for EGFR L858R mutation (exon 21, confirmed by both tissue NGS and liquid biopsy), with co-mutations in STK11 (Q37*, VAF 22%) and KEAP1 (G480V, VAF 19%), TP53 R248W, and CDKN2A deletion. PD-L1 TPS was 15% at diagnosis, though the STK11/KEAP1 co-mutations were considered unfavorable for immunotherapy response. Low-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 2.4) was noted at baseline. PD-L1 TPS 15%. ALK and ROS1 negative.

She underwent definitive concurrent chemoradiation (carbo AUC 2 / PTX 45 mg/m2 weekly x 6 weeks with 60 Gy in 30 fractions to left upper lobe primary and bilateral mediastinal/hilar nodal stations) with good tolerability -- notable toxicities were Grade 2 esophagitis and Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis symptoms, both of which resolved. Post-CRT restaging approximately 8 weeks after completion showed good response of the primary mass and nodal disease. She was then started on Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily as consolidation/maintenance per LAURA trial data, beginning approximately 10 weeks after completing CRT.

She initially did very well on Tagrisso. At her 6-month on-therapy assessment (roughly 3 months ago), she was tolerating Tagrisso well with only Grade 1 acneiform rash and intermittent Grade 1 diarrhea; cross-sectional imaging at that visit showed continued shrinkage of the residual left upper lobe opacity down to 1.3 x 1.0 cm. She was very pleased with her response and had returned to regular walking and some light gardening.

However, routine restaging CT CAP obtained approximately 2 weeks ago now demonstrates enlargement of the residual left upper lobe lesion (now 2.1 x 1.7 cm, up from 1.3 x 1.0 cm), a new right lower lobe nodule measuring 0.9 cm, and a new subcentimeter right adrenal nodule (0.7 cm) of indeterminate/suspicious character. Given these findings, an urgent brain MRI was obtained and confirmed two new enhancing intracranial lesions: a 1.4 cm right frontal lobe metastasis with surrounding vasogenic edema and a 0.7 cm left cerebellar hemisphere lesion. This represents clear multifocal progression after approximately 14 months of Tagrisso therapy. It is worth noting that this duration on Tagrisso in the Stage III setting is somewhat shorter than median PFS seen in LAURA, which we attribute in part to the STK11/KEAP1 co-mutations and the low-level MET amplification that was present at baseline.

CT-guided core biopsy of the right lower lobe nodule was performed last week to characterize resistance mechanism. Pathology confirms metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with her known primary (TTF-1 3+); no histologic transformation to SCLC. Molecular testing on the new biopsy reveals retained EGFR L858R, with acquisition of EGFR T790M (exon 20, VAF 28%) -- this is an unusual resistance mechanism for Tagrisso (more commonly seen with first/second-gen TKIs), occurring in ~10-15% of Tagrisso-resistant cases. Additionally, MET amplification has increased substantially (copy number ratio now 5.8 vs. 2.4 at diagnosis -- now meeting criteria for high-level amplification), suggesting MET-driven bypass as a co-resistance pathway. A new PIK3CA E545K mutation (VAF 12%) was also identified, of uncertain significance as a resistance driver. No C797S mutation. Concurrent ctDNA liquid biopsy confirmed EGFR L858R, T790M, and MET amplification.

The case was presented at our multidisciplinary thoracic tumor board today (attended by thoracic oncology, radiation oncology, neurosurgery, and neuroradiology). Discussion below.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
PERTINENT POSITIVE: Mild headaches over the past 2-3 weeks, which she initially attributed to stress/sinus issues. Fatigue, Grade 1 ongoing. Mild nausea, intermittent. Loose stools 1-2x/day (improved from prior on Tagrisso).
PERTINENT NEGATIVE: Denies focal neurological symptoms, vision changes, balance problems, seizures, or syncope. No hemoptysis. No worsening dyspnea or chest pain at rest. No significant weight loss over the past month. No bone pain. No dysuria or hematuria. No significant change in appetite.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
- osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg PO daily -- continuing for now, will be discontinued after SRS per plan below
- Folic acid 400 mcg PO daily (from prior peme supplementation -- can discontinue)
- Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (from prior radiation esophagitis -- patient continued on her own)
- Loperamide PRN (for diarrhea)
- Loratadine 10 mg PO daily PRN (allergic rhinitis)

ALLERGIES: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAM:
General: Alert, well-appearing female in no acute distress. Appears stated age. Well-nourished.
Vitals: BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, Temp 36.8 C, Weight 58.2 kg.
HEENT: PERRL, EOMI. No papilledema noted on fundoscopic exam (limited bedside exam). Mild acneiform papules on bilateral cheeks and chin, consistent with TKI-related rash.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rales, or rhonchi. Normal work of breathing.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. Normal bowel sounds.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor 5/5 in all extremities. Gait normal. Finger-to-nose without dysmetria. No pronator drift. No focal deficits identified.
Skin: Grade 1 acneiform rash as above.

---

RELEVANT LABS (from recent visit / on file):

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Yuen is a 63-year-old female with Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant NSCLC adenocarcinoma with now-confirmed systemic and intracranial disease progression after approximately 14 months of osi consolidation/maintenance following definitive CRT. Resistance biopsy confirms acquired T790M mutation (exon 20) and substantially increased MET amplification (copy number ratio 5.8) as co-resistance mechanisms. No histologic transformation. Two new brain metastases identified.

The case was reviewed in multidisciplinary tumor board today. The following plan was discussed and agreed upon:

1. BRAIN METASTASES -- STEREOTACTIC RADIOSURGERY (SRS):
   Both lesions are considered amenable to SRS (right frontal 1.4 cm, left cerebellar 0.7 cm) given their limited number, discrete nature, and absence of leptomeningeal disease. Radiation oncology and neurosurgery are in agreement that upfront SRS is preferred over whole brain radiation therapy (WBRT) to preserve neurocognitive function. This is especially important given the patient's former occupation as a schoolteacher and her strong expressed preference to maintain cognitive function. SRS will be planned on MRI-based targeting using the linear accelerator-based frameless system. She will continue osi until approximately 48 hours before the SRS procedure per our institutional protocol, then hold 72 hours post-treatment to minimize radiosensitization risk. Post-SRS short dexamethasone course (4 mg BID x 5 days then taper) will be prescribed. 
   -- Radiation oncology to schedule SRS within the next 7-10 days.

2. SYSTEMIC THERAPY -- TRANSITION TO PLATINUM/pem:
   Given multifocal systemic progression with complex resistance mechanisms (T790M + MET amplification), and no currently approved single-agent regimen targeting this combination outside of clinical trials, the plan is to transition to second-line platinum-based doublet chemotherapy. We will use Paraplatin (AUC 5) and Alimta (500 mg/m2) IV every 21 days starting approximately 3 weeks after SRS, once she has recovered and dexamethasone has been tapered.
   - Standard pem supplementation: folic acid 400 mcg PO daily (restart/continue) and vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM 7-10 days before cycle 1, then every 9 weeks.
   - Avastin will NOT be added given the prior thoracic radiation field and the theoretical risk of pulmonary hemorrhage.
   - Tagrisso to be discontinued when Paraplatin/Alimta is initiated.
   - We discussed the rationale for chemotherapy over continued EGFR TKI-based therapy given the MET amplification-driven bypass pathway, which is unlikely to respond to T790M-directed agents alone.

3. CLINICAL TRIAL CONSIDERATION:
   Given the MET amplification as a co-resistance mechanism, I have flagged this patient for potential enrollment in a Phase I/II trial investigating MET inhibitor-based strategies in combination with EGFR TKI in EGFR-mutant NSCLC with MET-amplified resistance. I will reach out to the trial team for a pre-screening discussion. This would be considered after achieving disease control on platinum/Alimta or in lieu of continued doublet therapy if eligibility is confirmed.

1. EGFR p.L858R (c.2573T>G), Exon 21
   Variant type: Missense substitution
   VAF: 41% (increased from 38% at initial diagnosis)
   Classification: Tier I — Strong clinical significance
   Interpretation: Activating driver mutation in EGFR exon 21. This is the founding oncogenic alteration identified at initial diagnosis and is retained at progression. The increased VAF relative to diagnostic specimen (38%) may reflect clonal enrichment in the context of osimertinib selective pressure or differences in tumor heterogeneity between sampled sites. EGFR L858R confers sensitivity to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors.

2. EGFR p.T790M (c.2369C>T), Exon 20
   Variant type: Missense substitution
   VAF: 28%
   Classification: Tier I — Strong clinical significance (NEWLY DETECTED; not present in diagnostic specimen)
   Interpretation: Acquired resistance mutation in EGFR exon 20. The T790M substitution (substitution of threonine for methionine at codon 790, the "gatekeeper" residue) is a well-characterized mechanism of acquired resistance to osimertinib, occurring in approximately 10–15% of osimertinib-resistant tumors. This mutation was ABSENT on prior diagnostic NGS (age 62) and is therefore interpreted as an acquired, osimertinib-resistant alteration. The T790M variant restores steric hindrance at the ATP-binding cleft of EGFR, reducing osimertinib binding affinity. The sub-clonal VAF (28% vs. L858R at 41%) is consistent with T790M arising as a secondary resistance alteration within a dominant L858R-positive clone. NOTE: EGFR C797S (tertiary resistance mutation conferring resistance to all third-generation EGFR TKIs) was NOT detected.

3. MET Amplification — HIGH-LEVEL
   Copy Number Ratio (tumor:ploidy-adjusted): 5.8
   (Increased from copy number ratio 2.4 at initial diagnostic NGS; now meeting threshold for high-level amplification)
   Classification: Tier I — Strong clinical significance (NEWLY HIGH-LEVEL; progression from low-level at diagnosis)
   Interpretation: High-level MET amplification is a well-recognized bypass resistance mechanism to EGFR TKI therapy, occurring as an acquired resistance alteration in approximately 15–20% of EGFR TKI-resistant NSCLC. In the context of osimertinib resistance, high-level MET amplification drives EGFR-independent signaling through the PI3K/AKT/mTOR and RAS/MAPK pathways, circumventing on-target EGFR inhibition. The progressive increase in MET copy number from low-level amplification at diagnosis (ratio 2.4) to high-level amplification at progression (ratio 5.8) suggests selective expansion of a MET-amplified subclone under EGFR TKI pressure. This co-occurring resistance mechanism (T790M + MET amplification) has been described in the literature and represents a therapeutically challenging co-resistance landscape.

4. PIK3CA p.E545K (c.1633G>A), Exon 9 (Helical domain)
   Variant type: Missense substitution
   VAF: 12%
   Classification: Tier II — Potential clinical significance (NEWLY DETECTED)
   Interpretation: Activating missense mutation in the helical domain of PIK3CA (the gene encoding the catalytic p110alpha subunit of PI3-kinase). PIK3CA E545K is a hotspot gain-of-function mutation that activates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway independently of upstream RTK signaling. This alteration was ABSENT on prior diagnostic NGS and is therefore acquired. The sub-clonal VAF (12%) suggests this may represent a minor resistance subclone. PIK3CA mutations have been described as co-resistance mechanisms in EGFR-mutant NSCLC, potentially contributing to osimertinib resistance through PI3K pathway activation independent of EGFR. Currently, there are no FDA-approved PI3K inhibitors specifically indicated for lung adenocarcinoma; however, alpelisib (PI3K alpha-selective inhibitor, FDA-approved for breast cancer) and other PI3K pathway inhibitors are under investigation in lung cancer clinical trials.

5. EGFR Amplification
   Copy Number Ratio: 4.4
   (Increased from 4.1 at initial diagnosis; stable/minor increase)
   Classification: Tier II — Potential clinical significance
   Interpretation: EGFR amplification persists, consistent with prior diagnostic findings. The degree of amplification has not substantially changed from the diagnostic specimen. EGFR amplification in the context of L858R may contribute to continued EGFR pathway dependence despite T790M-mediated resistance, and may influence response to next-line EGFR-directed therapies.

--- RETAINED FROM PRIOR DIAGNOSTIC SEQUENCING (TIER II–III) ---

6. STK11 p.Q37* (c.109C>T), Exon 1
   Variant type: Nonsense mutation (premature stop codon)
   VAF: 25% (stable from 22% at diagnosis)
   Classification: Tier II — Potential clinical significance
   Interpretation: Inactivating truncating mutation in STK11 (LKB1), a tumor suppressor gene involved in energy sensing and AMPK pathway regulation. This alteration is retained from the diagnostic specimen, consistent with a clonal, founding event. STK11 loss-of-function mutations are associated with resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy in NSCLC and may contribute to an immunologically cold tumor microenvironment. STK11 co-mutation has been associated with inferior outcomes to PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in KRAS- and EGFR-mutant NSCLC. Of note, PD-L1 TPS has decreased from 15% at initial diagnosis to 5% on this progression biopsy, consistent with the STK11-driven immunosuppressive phenotype.

7. KEAP1 p.G480V (c.1439G>T), Exon 5
   Variant type: Missense substitution (LOF predicted)
   VAF: 22% (stable from 19% at diagnosis)
   Classification: Tier II — Potential clinical significance
   Interpretation: Missense mutation in KEAP1, predicted to impair KEAP1-mediated ubiquitination and degradation of NRF2, leading to constitutive NRF2 pathway activation. STK11 and KEAP1 co-mutation (present in this patient) defines a genomically aggressive NSCLC subset with poor response to immunotherapy and potentially diminished antitumor immune surveillance. The retention and stable VAF of this alteration confirm it as an early, clonal event.

8. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T), Exon 7
   Variant type: Missense substitution (dominant-negative gain-of-function)
   VAF: 35% (slightly increased from 31% at diagnosis)
   Classification: Tier II — Potential clinical significance
   Interpretation: TP53 R248W is among the most common gain-of-function TP53 hotspot mutations, associated with dominant-negative inhibition of wild-type p53 and potential oncogenic gain-of-function activities. Retained from the diagnostic specimen. TP53 co-mutation in EGFR-mutant NSCLC has been associated with shorter progression-free survival on EGFR TKI therapy and with accelerated disease progression. The mild VAF increase from 31% to 35% may reflect clonal dynamics.

9. CDKN2A — Homozygous Deletion
   Copy Number: Loss (homozygous deletion confirmed, <0.3 copies)
   Classification: Tier II — Potential clinical significance
   Interpretation: Complete loss of CDKN2A (encoding p16/INK4A and p14/ARF) by homozygous deletion, retained from the diagnostic specimen. CDKN2A loss disrupts both RB pathway (via p16 loss) and MDM2-p53 pathway (via p14/ARF loss), contributing to unrestrained cell cycle progression and reduced apoptotic signaling. This alteration is consistent with its role as a clonal, early event.

CLINICAL INTERPRETATION AND ACTIONABILITY SUMMARY
================================================================================

ACTIONABILITY TIER SUMMARY

TIER I — FDA-APPROVED THERAPIES (in NSCLC or with approved companion diagnostic indication):

  * EGFR L858R + T790M [retained + acquired]:
    Although osimertinib (Tagrisso) is FDA-approved for EGFR T790M-positive NSCLC (second-line, post-1st/2nd-gen TKI), the T790M here arose as a resistance mechanism TO osimertinib (3rd-gen TKI), not as a primary resistance mechanism to 1st/2nd-gen inhibitors. Re-treatment with osimertinib monotherapy is therefore unlikely to be effective in standard dosing. The T790M alteration in this context may be addressable as part of combination regimens under investigation.

  * MET Amplification (high-level, CNR 5.8) [acquired]:
    High-level MET amplification may confer sensitivity to MET inhibitors. Capmatinib (Tabrecta) and tepotinib (Tepmetko) are FDA-approved for MET exon 14 skipping mutations in NSCLC; however, MET amplification as the primary indication currently lacks FDA approval for these agents as a standalone indication in lung cancer. MET amplification-directed therapy is being actively investigated in the context of EGFR TKI resistance, with several clinical trials evaluating MET inhibitor + EGFR TKI combinations.

TIER II — INVESTIGATIONAL / OFF-LABEL WITH CLINICAL EVIDENCE:

  * MET Amplification (high-level) as osimertinib resistance bypass mechanism:
    Multiple ongoing trials evaluate MET inhibitor (tepotinib, capmatinib, savolitinib) + osimertinib combinations in EGFR-mutant NSCLC with MET amplification-driven resistance. Early data suggest promising activity. CLINICAL TRIAL ENROLLMENT STRONGLY RECOMMENDED.

  * PIK3CA p.E545K [acquired, sub-clonal]:
    PI3K pathway inhibition (alpelisib) is under investigation in NSCLC. The sub-clonal nature of this alteration (VAF 12%) limits its current actionability as a primary therapeutic target but warrants monitoring. Presence of PIK3CA E545K may indicate partial PI3K pathway bypass resistance.

TIER III — BIOLOGIC/PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE, NO DIRECT CURRENT ACTIONABILITY:

  * STK11 p.Q37* — Marker of likely immunotherapy resistance; informs against PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitor use
  * KEAP1 p.G480V — Co-mutation with STK11 further supports immunotherapy resistance; may associate with resistance to platinum-based chemotherapy in some contexts
  * TP53 p.R248W — Prognostic; associated with aggressive disease biology in EGFR-mutant NSCLC
  * CDKN2A homozygous deletion — Prognostic; contributes to cell cycle dysregulation; CDK4/6 inhibitor combinations under investigation in NSCLC
  * EGFR amplification — May modulate EGFR pathway dependence; no independent actionability distinct from EGFR mutation status

================================================================================
SUMMARY AND CLINICAL RECOMMENDATIONS
================================================================================

This specimen, obtained at disease progression following osimertinib consolidation therapy for Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, demonstrates a complex acquired resistance landscape. Two co-occurring resistance mechanisms have been identified:

  1. EGFR T790M acquisition (VAF 28%) — on-target resistance via gatekeeper mutation
  2. MET high-level amplification (CNR 5.8, increased from 2.4 at diagnosis) — off-target bypass resistance

In addition, a newly acquired PIK3CA E545K mutation (sub-clonal, VAF 12%) may represent an additional emerging resistance subclone. All founding driver alterations (EGFR L858R, STK11 Q37*, KEAP1 G480V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A deletion) are retained.

The co-existence of T790M and MET amplification as dual resistance mechanisms represents a therapeutic challenge, as neither alteration alone may be sufficient to drive complete resistance and each may be differentially addressable. This molecular profile is consistent with published EGFR TKI resistance profiling data and has direct implications for clinical trial eligibility — particularly trials evaluating EGFR TKI re-treatment in combination with MET inhibition.

TMB remains low (8 mutations/Mb) and MSI-stable status, together with STK11/KEAP1 co-mutations, argues strongly against immunotherapy-based approaches.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Margaret Yuen is a 63-year-old Chinese-American woman with a well-established history of Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0, AJCC 8th edition) left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma harboring EGFR L858R (exon 21) with co-mutations in STK11, KEAP1, and TP53. She initially presented approximately 14 months ago with a 3.8 cm primary left upper lobe mass and contralateral mediastinal nodal involvement. She received definitive concurrent chemoradiation — carboplatin AUC 2/paclitaxel 45 mg/m2 weekly with thoracic IMRT to 60 Gy in 30 fractions — followed by consolidation with osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily, per LAURA trial protocol, given her EGFR driver mutation. She had a solid partial response on her post-CRT restaging CT, with the primary lesion shrinking from 3.8 to 1.9 cm, and continued to have gradual further decrease to 1.3 cm on her 9-month on-osimertinib scan.

She now presents following her most recent surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, obtained at approximately 14 months after osimertinib initiation, which showed enlargement of the residual left upper lobe lesion (now 2.1 x 1.7 cm, up from 1.3 x 1.0 cm), a new right lower lobe nodule measuring 0.9 cm, and a new right adrenal nodule measuring 0.7 cm, all highly suspicious for progressive disease. An urgent MRI brain was subsequently obtained given concern for systemic spread, and this demonstrated two new enhancing intracranial lesions: a 1.4 cm right frontal lobe metastasis with approximately 2.8 cm of surrounding vasogenic edema, and a 0.7 cm left cerebellar hemisphere metastasis. No leptomeningeal enhancement was identified. Of note, the previously noted right parietal extra-axial 4 mm lesion remains stable and is not consistent with metastatic disease.

Given these findings, she underwent re-biopsy of the new right lower lobe nodule, which confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with known primary lung origin. Crucially, reflex EGFR testing on this specimen confirmed retention of the L858R mutation along with a newly acquired T790M resistance mutation (exon 20). Comprehensive tissue NGS on this specimen additionally identified high-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 5.8, increased from 2.4 at initial diagnosis), a new PIK3CA E545K mutation, and retention of all prior co-mutations (STK11, KEAP1, TP53, CDKN2A deletion). No EGFR C797S was detected. Liquid biopsy confirmed concordant findings.

Ms. Yuen reports mild intermittent headaches over the past 2–3 weeks, which she had attributed to tension headaches. In retrospect, she wonders whether these may have been related to the intracranial lesions. She reports no focal neurological symptoms — no weakness, numbness, vision changes, or gait instability. She denies seizures. She has noted increasing fatigue over the past 4–6 weeks, which she has been managing with daytime rest. She reports no new cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, or shortness of breath beyond her baseline mild post-radiation dyspnea.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Yuen is a 63-year-old woman with Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, now with radiographic and pathologic evidence of osimertinib resistance and disease progression affecting the lung (left upper lobe, new right lower lobe), right adrenal gland, and CNS (right frontal and left cerebellar metastases). The molecular landscape at progression reveals a complex resistance biology: acquired EGFR T790M (exon 20) co-existing with high-level MET amplification as likely convergent bypass resistance mechanisms, alongside her pre-existing unfavorable co-mutations in STK11 and KEAP1.

This case was presented and discussed in detail at today's thoracic oncology multidisciplinary tumor board, attended by medical oncology, radiation oncology, neuroradiology, and neurosurgery.

1. BRAIN METASTASES — Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS):
Board consensus is to proceed with SRS to both intracranial lesions (right frontal 1.4 cm; left cerebellar 0.7 cm) rather than whole-brain radiation therapy (WBRT). Given a limited number of lesions (two), good performance status, and the critical importance of preserving neurocognitive function in this relatively young, engaged patient, SRS is strongly preferred. Neurosurgery confirms no indication for surgical resection at this time. Radiation oncology will plan frameless, linac-based SRS. Osimertinib will be held 48 hours prior to and 72 hours following SRS to minimize radiosensitization risk per institutional protocol. Dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily to be started peri-procedurally and tapered over 1–2 weeks post-SRS for management of peri-tumoral edema, particularly given the right frontal lesion edema burden. Levetiracetam not currently indicated in absence of seizure activity, per neurosurgery recommendations.

2. SYSTEMIC PROGRESSION — Second-Line Chemotherapy:
Following SRS, osimertinib will be permanently discontinued given confirmed acquired resistance (T790M/MET amplification). Given the STK11/KEAP1 co-mutation profile unfavorable for checkpoint inhibitor response, and low PD-L1 (5% on re-biopsy), immunotherapy is not favored. The plan is to transition to platinum-based doublet chemotherapy with carboplatin (AUC 5, day 1) and pemetrexed (500 mg/m2, day 1) every 21 days. Bevacizumab will not be added given prior thoracic radiation and concerns regarding pulmonary hemorrhage risk. Standard pemetrexed supplementation (folic acid 400 mcg daily, vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks) will be initiated 7 days prior to first cycle per protocol.

3. MET AMPLIFICATION / RESISTANCE MECHANISM — Clinical Trial Referral:
The high-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 5.8 at progression, up from 2.4 at diagnosis) is a compelling acquired resistance mechanism for which there is no currently FDA-approved combination in this setting. The molecular tumor board has identified a Phase I/II clinical trial investigating tepotinib in combination with osimertinib in EGFR-mutant NSCLC with MET amplification post-EGFR TKI progression as a potentially highly relevant option. We discussed this with Ms. Yuen, who expresses strong interest in trial participation after platinum-doublet chemotherapy, and potentially sooner depending on trial eligibility timeline. Clinical trials coordinator will reach out to complete eligibility screening.

4. SUPPORTIVE CARE:
Prophylactic antiemetics for upcoming chemotherapy. Continue PPI. Monitor CBC/CMP each cycle. Assess for and manage chemotherapy-related nausea, myelosuppression, and fatigue proactively. Continue amlodipine at current dose. Reinforce nutritional support given treatment burden ahead; dietitian referral placed.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Ms. Yuen and her husband were extensively counseled today regarding the current disease status, the rationale for each component of the planned approach, and realistic expectations. She is coping well emotionally, with good family support, and is appropriately determined. Social work referral offered and accepted for ongoing support during this more intensive treatment phase. Patient advocacy resources reinforced.

ECOG Performance Status: 1. Cancer present; systemic and CNS progression after osimertinib therapy.

Follow-up: SRS scheduled with radiation oncology within the next 7–10 days. First cycle carboplatin/pemetrexed to be initiated approximately 2–3 weeks post-SRS pending recovery. Laboratory work and baseline PFTs to be obtained prior to chemotherapy initiation.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Histologic sections of Specimen A demonstrate fragments of lung parenchyma with infiltration by a malignant epithelial neoplasm. The tumor is composed predominantly of glandular structures arranged in an acinar pattern (estimated 60% of tumor) with areas of lepidic growth along alveolar walls (estimated 30%). A minor component shows solid nests of tumor cells without gland formation (estimated 10%). The acinar component shows well-formed gland-like structures lined by columnar to cuboidal cells with moderate nuclear enlargement and prominent nucleoli. The lepidic component demonstrates neoplastic cells spreading along the alveolar framework without stromal invasion in those zones, though the overall lesion is classified as invasive given the predominant acinar component. The solid component shows sheets of cells with moderate nuclear pleomorphism, coarse chromatin, and occasional mitotic figures (approximately 4 per 10 high-power fields). No keratinization or intercellular bridges are identified. Intranuclear pseudoinclusions are focally present. Mucin production is minimal; periodic acid-Schiff staining highlights sparse intracytoplasmic mucin in a subset of cells.

The stroma is desmoplastic with focal inflammatory infiltrate. No lymphovascular invasion is identified within the biopsy specimen. No perineural invasion is seen. No necrosis. Background lung parenchyma demonstrates mild chronic interstitial changes and scattered alveolar macrophages with anthracotic pigment, consistent with prior smoking history.

The morphologic and immunohistochemical findings confirm a primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma with acinar-predominant architecture. INDICATION FOR STUDY: Initial oncologic staging. 62-year-old female with newly diagnosed left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant, confirmed by CT-guided core needle biopsy. Study requested for whole-body metabolic staging prior to treatment planning.

Images were acquired approximately 62 minutes after injection from the vertex to the mid-thighs using a dedicated PET/CT system (Siemens Biograph mCT). Attenuation correction was performed using low-dose CT. A separate diagnostic CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was acquired with intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL, portal venous phase). Oral contrast was not administered. Images were reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes with fused PET/CT display. Maximum intensity projection (MIP) images reviewed.

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:

No focal area of markedly abnormal FDG uptake is identified in the visualized intracranial structures. No cervical or supraclavicular nodal hypermetabolism is identified. No pathologically enlarged cervical lymph nodes by CT morphology.

THORAX:

Primary Lesion:
There is a spiculated, lobulated mass in the posterior segment of the left upper lobe measuring approximately 3.8 x 2.9 cm in axial dimension. The mass demonstrates intense, heterogeneous FDG avidity with a peak standardized uptake value (SUVmax) of 12.4. The margins are irregular with associated pleural tethering noted along the posterior pleural surface. Multiple hypermetabolic lymph nodes are identified in the mediastinum and hila bilaterally.

- Right paratracheal region (station 4R): A cluster of enlarged, FDG-avid lymph nodes is present, with the dominant node measuring approximately 1.9 cm in short axis. SUVmax 9.1. The contralateral (right-sided) involvement in the setting of a left-sided primary constitutes N3 disease.

- Subcarinal region (station 7): An enlarged lymph node measures 1.4 cm in short axis with SUVmax 7.3, consistent with active nodal metastatic involvement.

- Left hilum (station 10L/11L): Mildly enlarged hilar nodal soft tissue demonstrates FDG avidity with SUVmax 5.2, compatible with nodal disease in the ipsilateral hilum.

- Left lower paratracheal region (station 4L): Borderline nodal tissue with mildly increased tracer uptake, below threshold for definitive involvement; cannot exclude early involvement.

- Aortopulmonary window (station 5): No discrete hypermetabolic node identified; subcentimeter soft tissue present without convincing uptake above background.

Lungs (remainder):
No additional pulmonary nodules with significant FDG uptake are identified beyond the primary lesion and satellite nodule described above. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

HEART AND GREAT VESSELS:

Retroperitoneum and abdominal/pelvic lymph nodes: No retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or pelvic lymphadenopathy. No pathologically enlarged nodes by size or metabolic criteria.

The satellite nodule is classified within the same lobe (T3 by AJCC 8th edition criteria).

2. Hypermetabolic contralateral right paratracheal (station 4R) lymphadenopathy (dominant node 1.9 cm, SUVmax 9.1) and subcarinal (station 7) nodal disease (1.4 cm, SUVmax 7.3), representing contralateral mediastinal involvement. MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

EXAMINATION: MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast enhancement

INDICATION: 62-year-old female with newly diagnosed Stage IIIB left upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant. Study obtained for CNS staging prior to initiation of definitive concurrent chemoradiation therapy.

COMPARISON: No prior brain MRI available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet. Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1, axial T1, axial T2, axial FLAIR, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, axial gradient echo (GRE/SWI susceptibility-weighted imaging), and axial T1 fat-saturated sequences pre- and post-intravenous gadolinium contrast (gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg). Post-contrast sequences include axial, coronal, and sagittal T1 fat-saturated images with thin-section 1 mm isotropic volumetric acquisition.

---

FINDINGS:

Parenchyma:
The cerebral and cerebellar hemispheres demonstrate normal signal intensity and morphology. There is no evidence of focal parenchymal signal abnormality on T2 or FLAIR sequences to suggest edema, infiltrating process, or ischemic change. No cortical or subcortical restricted diffusion to indicate acute or subacute infarction. The cerebral white matter is grossly unremarkable without significant confluent periventricular or deep white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities beyond very mild, scattered punctate foci in the deep white matter bilaterally, a nonspecific finding within normal limits for patient age. No hemorrhage identified on susceptibility-weighted imaging.

Post-Contrast Enhancement:
Following intravenous gadolinium administration, there is no abnormal parenchymal, leptomeningeal, or dural enhancement. No ring-enhancing lesions. No nodular enhancing foci in the cerebral cortex, subcortical white matter, or posterior fossa structures. The basal cisterns and sulci enhance in a symmetric, normal physiologic pattern without thickening or nodularity suggestive of leptomeningeal carcinomatosis. The cranial nerves assessed at the skull base show normal enhancement pattern bilaterally.

Extra-Axial Spaces:
There is a small, well-circumscribed, homogeneously T2-isointense to hypointense extra-axial lesion at the right parietal convexity measuring approximately 4 mm in greatest dimension. This lesion demonstrates homogeneous enhancement following contrast administration and appears to follow the contour of the inner calvarial table with a broad dural base. The overlying calvarium shows no evidence of hyperostosis. There is no associated parenchymal edema, mass effect, or sulcal effacement. The morphology and imaging characteristics are most consistent with a small meningioma, likely WHO Grade I given its size and benign imaging features. This is considered an incidental finding. No other extra-axial lesions identified.

Ventricles and Cisterns:
The lateral, third, and fourth ventricles are normal in size and configuration. No hydrocephalus. The basal cisterns are patent and symmetric. No evidence of transependymal flow. Midline structures are in normal position. No midline shift.

Sellar and Parasellar Region:
The pituitary gland demonstrates normal size and morphology. The infundibulum is midline. No sellar or parasellar mass.

Posterior Fossa:
The brainstem demonstrates normal signal and morphology on all sequences. The cerebellum is symmetric with normal folia. The cerebellar tonsils are at the expected position. No posterior fossa mass or extra-axial collection.

Vascular Structures:
The major intracranial vascular flow voids appear normal in caliber and distribution on standard sequences. No obvious vascular malformation identified. Evaluation of intracranial vessels is limited on this non-angiographic study.

Bone and Soft Tissues:
Calvarial signal is unremarkable without evidence of osseous metastatic lesions. Visualized scalp soft tissues are unremarkable. The paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No intracranial metastatic disease identified. No parenchymal, leptomeningeal, or dural enhancing lesions to suggest CNS involvement by known primary lung adenocarcinoma.

2. No acute intracranial abnormality. No infarct, hemorrhage, or hydrocephalus.

3. No midline shift or mass effect.

4. Incidental small 4 mm extra-axial enhancing lesion at the right parietal convexity with imaging characteristics most consistent with a small meningioma. Given its small size and lack of associated mass effect or edema, this is considered clinically inconsequential at this time. Routine follow-up imaging is appropriate to confirm stability.

5. Minimal, scattered punctate deep white matter T2/FLAIR signal changes, nonspecific and likely related to age/microvascular etiology.

INTERPRETATION AND CLINICAL COMMENTARY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
This 62-year-old female with Stage IIIB lung adenocarcinoma harbors an EGFR exon 21 L858R activating point mutation (VAF 38%) as the dominant oncogenic driver. This is the most common EGFR sensitizing mutation (comprising approximately 40-45% of all EGFR-mutant NSCLCs) and is associated with robust clinical responses to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors. Notably, EGFR amplification is also present (CNR 4.1), which may serve to further amplify oncogene signaling.

The mutational co-landscape is notable and clinically important:

1. STK11 p.Q37* (VAF 22%): This early truncating nonsense mutation results in complete loss of LKB1 kinase function. STK11/LKB1 is a master regulator of cellular metabolism and is a well-validated tumor suppressor in NSCLC. STK11 mutations co-occur in approximately 15-25% of KRAS-mutant and a smaller fraction of EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinomas. Loss of LKB1 reprograms the tumor microenvironment toward an immunosuppressed, neutrophil-rich state, which has been consistently associated with primary resistance to PD-1 and PD-L1 checkpoint blockade. While the patient's PD-L1 TPS is 15% (low positive), the STK11 mutation substantially diminishes the expected benefit from immunotherapy.

2. KEAP1 p.G480V (VAF 19%): KEAP1 encodes a negative regulator of NRF2, the master transcription factor for antioxidant and cytoprotective gene expression. Loss-of-function KEAP1 mutations lead to constitutive NRF2 activation, conferring resistance to oxidative stress (including that induced by chemotherapy and radiation), and have been independently associated with poor outcomes in NSCLC. The concurrent presence of both STK11 and KEAP1 mutations in a single tumor — as seen here — represents a particularly unfavorable co-mutation signature in lung adenocarcinoma, strongly predicting resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy and associated with a more aggressive clinical course.

3. TP53 p.R248W (VAF 31%): This is one of the most common gain-of-function TP53 hotspot mutations (R248W and R248Q are the most frequently mutated TP53 codons in human cancer). Dominant-negative TP53 mutations disrupt wild-type p53 tetramer function and may contribute to genomic instability. Co-mutation of TP53 with EGFR in lung adenocarcinoma is common (~30-40% of EGFR-mutant cases) and does not substantially alter EGFR TKI sensitivity in most studies.

4. CDKN2A homozygous deletion: Loss of CDKN2A abrogates both p16/INK4A (CDK4/6 inhibitory) and p14/ARF (MDM2-p53 regulatory) functions. In the context of the co-occurring TP53 mutation, complete loss of this checkpoint axis may contribute to accelerated cell cycle progression.

5. MET low-level amplification (CNR 2.4): This finding is at the borderline of interpretive significance. True high-level MET amplification (CNR ≥5) is a known primary resistance mechanism to EGFR TKI therapy. At this level, the clinical significance is less certain — polysomy of chromosome 7 (which harbors both EGFR at 7p11.2 and MET at 7q31.2) could account for a co-amplification pattern. This finding should be reassessed at the time of any disease progression on EGFR TKI therapy, as clonal selection pressure may drive emergence of high-level MET amplification as an acquired resistance mechanism.

IMPORTANT NEGATIVE FINDINGS: The absence of KRAS, BRAF, ALK, ROS1, RET, MET exon 14 skipping, ERBB2, and NTRK alterations is consistent with classical EGFR-driven adenocarcinoma biology. The absence of T790M at baseline is expected. TMB is low (6 mut/Mb), consistent with the relatively low mutational burden typical of EGFR-mutant NSCLC in former light smokers. No germline pathogenic variants were identified.

THORACIC ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE
Multidisciplinary Tumor Board / New Patient Oncology Consultation

**REFERRING PROVIDER:** Pulmonology / Interventional Pulmonology
**REASON FOR CONSULT:** New diagnosis of Stage IIIB non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma; management recommendations requested

---

**CHIEF COMPLAINT:**
Ms. Yuen is a 62-year-old Chinese-American woman and retired schoolteacher referred for evaluation and management of a newly diagnosed left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, Stage IIIB, following CT-guided biopsy that returned positive for malignancy approximately 2 weeks ago. She presents today accompanied by her daughter.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:**
Ms. Yuen initially came to medical attention approximately 6 weeks ago with a 2-month history of progressive exertional dyspnea and a new, persistent dry cough. She denied hemoptysis at that time, though reports one episode of blood-tinged sputum approximately 3 weeks prior to this visit. She also endorses approximately 8 lbs of unintentional weight loss over the past 3 months and mild left-sided chest discomfort, described as a dull ache rather than pleuritic pain.

A chest radiograph obtained by her primary care physician showed a left upper lobe opacity. This prompted CT chest with contrast, which revealed a 3.8 cm spiculated left upper lobe mass with a smaller satellite nodule in the same lobe, and enlarged mediastinal and contralateral right-sided lymph nodes. The patient was subsequently referred to interventional pulmonology, where CT-guided core needle biopsy of the left upper lobe mass was performed and returned invasive adenocarcinoma with TTF-1 positivity, consistent with primary lung origin.

For staging purposes, PET-CT whole body was obtained, demonstrating hypermetabolic activity in the primary left upper lobe mass (SUVmax 12.4), the satellite nodule (SUVmax 6.8), contralateral right paratracheal (4R) nodes (largest 1.9 cm, SUVmax 9.1), subcarinal station 7 (SUVmax 7.3), and left hilar nodes. No evidence of distant organ metastases was identified. Brain MRI with and without contrast showed no intracranial metastatic disease, though an incidental 4 mm right parietal extra-axial lesion was noted (likely small meningioma, no mass effect). Overall staging: T3 N3 M0, Stage IIIB per AJCC 8th edition.

Molecular profiling from the biopsy specimen was notable for EGFR L858R mutation in exon 21 (VAF 38% on tissue NGS), TP53 R248W co-mutation (VAF 31%), STK11 Q37* nonsense mutation (VAF 22%), and KEAP1 G480V (VAF 19%). Low-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 2.4) and EGFR amplification (copy number ratio 4.1) were also identified. KRAS, BRAF, ALK, ROS1, RET, and MET exon 14 skipping alterations were all absent. TMB is low at 6 mutations/Mb, MSI-stable. PD-L1 TPS 15% by 22C3 PharmDx.

This case was brought to the thoracic multidisciplinary tumor board (MTB) today, with representation from thoracic medical oncology (myself), radiation oncology, thoracic surgery, thoracic radiology, and pulmonology.

**IMAGING REVIEW:**
PET-CT and brain MRI reviewed in detail at today's tumor board with thoracic radiology present. See prior reports for detailed findings. In summary: 3.8 cm hypermetabolic left upper lobe primary with satellite nodule, hypermetabolic bilateral mediastinal and contralateral right-sided nodal disease (N3), no distant solid organ or bony metastases, no CNS disease. The right paratracheal and subcarinal stations are the most prominent nodal sites. The overall nodal distribution is felt to be definitively N3 given contralateral mediastinal involvement.

---

**MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION AND ASSESSMENT:**

Ms. Yuen is a 62-year-old woman with a new diagnosis of Stage IIIB left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, T3 N3 M0. The case was presented at today's thoracic MTB and discussed in detail.

**Surgical candidacy:** Thoracic surgery reviewed the imaging and confirmed that resection is not appropriate given N3 contralateral mediastinal involvement. She is not a surgical candidate.

**EGFR mutation and treatment strategy:** The EGFR L858R exon 21 mutation is the dominant oncogenic driver. This is a very strong targetable alteration. We had extended discussion regarding the optimal treatment approach for Stage IIIB EGFR-mutant NSCLC. The standard of care in EGFR-unselected Stage III unresectable disease has been concurrent chemoradiation followed by durvalumab (Imfinzi) maintenance per the PACIFIC trial. However, in EGFR-mutant patients, the PACIFIC benefit has been substantially less clear -- multiple analyses suggest EGFR-mutant patients derive little to no benefit from durvalumab, and given her STK11 and KEAP1 co-mutations, which are associated with an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment and reduced responsiveness to PD-1/PD-L1 blockade, we would not expect meaningful benefit from immunotherapy in this patient. Her PD-L1 of 15% is modest and in the context of these co-mutations is not reassuring.

The LAURA trial data were reviewed. This Phase III study examined osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily as consolidation therapy after definitive concurrent chemoradiation in patients with unresectable Stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC. The data showed a striking improvement in progression-free survival with osimertinib consolidation vs. placebo in this setting (median PFS approximately 39 months vs. 6 months). The trial has changed our approach to this specific population. After discussion with the team and review of the totality of evidence, we are recommending definitive concurrent chemoradiation followed by osimertinib consolidation/maintenance, rather than the durvalumab-based approach.

**Chemotherapy/radiation plan:** Concurrent carboplatin AUC 2 and paclitaxel 45 mg/m2 weekly x6 weeks (weekly low-dose radiosensitizing regimen) with thoracic radiation therapy, 60 Gy in 30 fractions using IMRT/IGRT technique. Radiation oncology has reviewed the case and is in agreement; they will design the treatment plan to cover the primary, satellite nodule, and involved mediastinal/hilar nodes bilaterally. MLD and V20 constraints are felt to be achievable. Following completion of concurrent CRT, assuming acceptable tolerance and no progression, she will transition to osimertinib 80 mg orally once daily as consolidation. The STK11/KEAP1 co-mutations were noted as potentially biologically unfavorable but should not preclude an EGFR-TKI approach.

**Patient discussion:** The treatment rationale, goals, expected duration, and side effects were discussed at length with Ms. Yuen and her daughter. She understands this is not a curative approach in the conventional sense given N3 disease, but that the goal is long-term disease control, and that some patients with EGFR-mutant Stage III NSCLC do very well with this strategy. She is agreeable to proceed. She asked about hair loss -- reassured that the weekly low-dose paclitaxel regimen is associated with less alopecia than full-dose paclitaxel. Dysphagia and esophagitis risks from concurrent thoracic RT were reviewed; she will be counseled by the radiation team as well. She will need nutritional counseling given pre-existing mild weight loss.

---

**PLAN:**
1. Proceed with definitive concurrent chemoradiation: carboplatin AUC 2 + paclitaxel 45 mg/m2 weekly x6 weeks with IMRT thoracic radiation 60 Gy/30 fx. Radiation oncology to complete planning.
2. Following CRT, restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast at approximately 6-8 weeks post-treatment completion.
3. If no progression, initiate osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily as consolidation/maintenance per LAURA trial schema.
4. Baseline labs prior to starting treatment: CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH, LDH, CEA.
5. Dental evaluation prior to radiation (if not already completed).
6. Nutrition consultation for pre-treatment optimization.
7. Pulmonary rehabilitation referral discussed -- patient will consider.
8. Repeat brain MRI not needed urgently given recent negative study; follow-up annually or per clinical indication.
9. Incidental right parietal extra-axial lesion (likely meningioma) to be followed with annual MRI -- no intervention at this time.
10. Social work and oncology nursing involvement for psychosocial support and treatment education.
11. Return to thoracic oncology clinic within 1-2 weeks to confirm readiness to start and review treatment plan details.
